What counts as a “normal” P/E often reflects how the market views a company’s growth outlook and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while lower growth or higher risk can lead to a lower one.

data.title: Tosei (TSE:8923) Net Margin At 15.6% Reinforces Bullish Community Narratives
data.description:
Tosei (TSE:8923) has just opened its 2026 financial year with Q1 results that come on the back of a quarter where revenue was ¥10.7b and basic EPS was ¥7.30, rounding out a year in which trailing twelve month revenue reached ¥94.7b and EPS was ¥152.17. Over recent quarters the company has seen revenue move from ¥46.1b in Q1 2025 to ¥19.9b in Q2, ¥17.9b in Q3 and ¥10.7b in Q4, with basic EPS stepping from ¥84.36 to ¥41.77, ¥18.76 and ¥7.30 over the same stretch. This sets up a fresh quarter where investors are focused on how net profit margins are holding up across the portfolio.

See our full analysis for Tosei.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up against the prevailing narratives about Tosei’s growth profile and risk reward trade off among Simply Wall St investors.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Margins Hold Firm With 15.6% Net Profit

  • Over the last 12 months, Tosei converted ¥94,688 million of revenue into ¥14,754 million of net income, giving a 15.6% net margin compared with 14.6% in the prior year.
  • What stands out for a more bullish angle is that this 15.6% margin sits alongside 23.1% earnings growth over the year and 19.8% average annual earnings growth over five years, which strongly supports the view of Tosei as a real estate value creator while also showing that recent profit strength is grounded in actual earnings, not just cost cutting or one off items.
    • The trailing twelve month revenue line moved from ¥82,191.8 million in 2024 Q4 to ¥94,688 million in 2025 Q4, while net income went from ¥11,985.2 million to ¥14,754 million over the same span.
    • This combination of higher revenue and higher net income backs the idea that Tosei’s value up projects and diversified real estate activities are translating into more profit per yen of sales.

Valuation Signals With 11.2x P/E

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 11.2x, below the Japan real estate industry average of 12.2x and the peer average of 15.7x, and the current ¥1,697 share price sits below the DCF fair value estimate of ¥2,691.20.
  • For investors weighing a bullish argument, this combination of a lower P/E and a discount to a DCF fair value of ¥2,691.20 is heavily grounded in the reported earnings profile, where 23.1% earnings growth over the last year and a 15.6% net margin suggest the business has been generating profits that are not fully reflected in the current multiple.
    • Bulls often highlight that earnings have compounded at 19.8% per year over five years, which makes an 11.2x P/E look conservative compared with peers on 15.7x.
    • At the same time, forecasts of 6.81% annual earnings growth and 7% revenue growth indicate expectations are more measured than the past track record, so any bullish case has to reconcile the strong history with these more moderate projections.
On this kind of setup, where the current P/E undercuts peers and the share price sits below an indicated DCF fair value, many investors want to see how a detailed bull or bear story fits around the numbers, not just the headline multiples. Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives.

Cash Flow Coverage Flags Around Dividends and Debt

  • Tosei’s dividend yield sits at 3.24%, but that payout is not well covered by free cash flow, and debt is not well covered by operating cash flow according to the trailing twelve month analysis.
  • Critics who lean more bearish point to this cash flow profile as a key financial risk, because even with a 15.6% net margin and ¥14,754 million of net income on ¥94,688 million of revenue, the quality of cash coming in and going out matters for how robust that 3.24% dividend and the current balance sheet really are.
    • The tension here is that strong reported earnings and margin levels can sit alongside weaker cash coverage, which means income investors may not be fully protected if cash generation lags accounting profit.
    • For more cautious holders, the combination of debt not being well covered by operating cash flow and dividends not being covered by free cash flow is likely to be weighed just as heavily as the valuation discount or past earnings growth.

data.title: There May Be Reason For Hope In Aumann's (ETR:AAG) Disappointing Earnings
data.description:
The market for Aumann AG's (ETR:AAG) shares didn't move much after it posted weak earnings recently. We did some digging, and we believe the earnings are stronger than they seem.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Zooming In On Aumann's Earnings

In high finance, the key ratio used to measure how well a company converts reported profits into free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio (from cashflow). The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. The ratio shows us how much a company's profit exceeds its FCF.

That means a negative accrual ratio is a good thing, because it shows that the company is bringing in more free cash flow than its profit would suggest. That is not intended to imply we should worry about a positive accrual ratio, but it's worth noting where the accrual ratio is rather high. That's because some academic studies have suggested that high accruals ratios tend to lead to lower profit or less profit growth.

Over the twelve months to December 2025, Aumann recorded an accrual ratio of -0.35. That indicates that its free cash flow quite significantly exceeded its statutory profit. Indeed, in the last twelve months it reported free cash flow of €34m, well over the €15.7m it reported in profit. Aumann's free cash flow improved over the last year, which is generally good to see.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

Our Take On Aumann's Profit Performance

As we discussed above, Aumann's accrual ratio indicates strong conversion of profit to free cash flow, which is a positive for the company. Because of this, we think Aumann's underlying earnings potential is as good as, or possibly even better, than the statutory profit makes it seem! And on top of that, its earnings per share have grown at an extremely impressive rate over the last three years. Of course, we've only just scratched the surface when it comes to analysing its earnings; one could also consider margins, forecast growth, and return on investment, among other factors. With this in mind, we wouldn't consider investing in a stock unless we had a thorough understanding of the risks. In terms of investment risks, we've identified 1 warning sign with Aumann, and understanding it should be part of your investment process.

Today we've zoomed in on a single data point to better understand the nature of Aumann's profit. But there is always more to discover if you are capable of focussing your mind on minutiae.   • Over the past year, Intel has posted a 159.5% return, with 28.9% year to date, 17.0% over the last 30 days and 23.3% over the past week, which has clearly shifted how the market is treating the stock.
  • These moves sit against a backdrop of ongoing attention on chipmakers as key suppliers to data centers, personal computers and broader technology infrastructure. Recent headlines have focused on the sector's role in supporting AI hardware build outs and capacity expansions, which keeps Intel in the conversation whenever investors reassess large semiconductor names.
  • Right now Intel holds a valuation score of 3 out of 6 on Simply Wall St. The rest of this article will break down how different valuation approaches line up on that score, before finishing with a more holistic way to think about what the stock might be worth.

Intel delivered 159.5% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Semiconductor industry.

Approach 1: Intel Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars to estimate what the whole business might be worth per share.

For Intel, Simply Wall St uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model. The latest twelve months show free cash flow of a loss of about US$11.5b. Analyst and extrapolated projections move this to positive territory over time, with forecast free cash flow of US$4.3b in 2029 and further projected growth through 2035 based on the model’s assumptions. These later years are not analyst estimates; they are extrapolations built into the DCF framework.

Pulling all of those projected cash flows together and discounting them back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of about US$33.39 per share. Against a share price of roughly US$50.78, this implies the stock is about 52.1% above the DCF estimate, which points to a valuation that is rich on this cash flow view.

Result: OVERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Intel.

Approach 2: Intel Price vs Sales

For companies where earnings are less useful, such as when profits are volatile or negative, the P/S ratio can be a practical way to think about valuation because it anchors the share price to revenue, which is often more stable.

Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/S depending on what they expect for future growth and how risky the business appears. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher “normal” multiple, while slower expected growth or higher risk typically lines up with a lower one.

Intel currently trades on a P/S of 4.82x. That sits below the Semiconductor industry average of 5.83x and also below the peer group average of 9.30x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Intel is 6.20x, which is its proprietary estimate of what a reasonable P/S might be given factors such as earnings growth, industry, profit margin, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the sector because it adjusts for Intel’s own growth profile, risk characteristics and profitability instead of assuming that every chipmaker should trade on the same multiple. On this basis, Intel’s current P/S of 4.82x sits below the Fair Ratio of 6.20x, which indicates that the shares may be undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

🐂 Intel Bull Case

Revenue growth assumption: 10%

  • Sees a refreshed CPU roadmap across PCs and data centers, plus foundry, Mobileye and other units, as setting Intel up for higher revenue, a 22% net margin and EPS of US$4.80 by 2027.

data.description:

Recent coverage has focused on how expectations around that positioning feed into optimism, but also into debates about what constitutes a reasonable price.
  • Even so, Arm currently scores just 1 out of 6 on our valuation checks. The next sections will walk through the traditional valuation methods investors often look at, before finishing with a framework that can help you build your own, more complete view of value.

Approach 1: Arm Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what all of Arm Holdings' future cash flows are worth in today's US$.

For Arm, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is around US$1.16b. Analysts and extrapolated estimates point to projected free cash flow of US$6.38b in the year to March 2031, with a detailed path of annual projections between 2026 and 2035 that Simply Wall St discounts back to today.

Adding up those discounted cash flows gives an estimated intrinsic value of about US$72.44 per share. Against a recent share price of around US$148.77, the model suggests the stock is about 105.4% above this DCF estimate. This indicates that a lot of optimism is already embedded in the price.

Approach 2: Arm Holdings Price vs Sales

For profitable companies where earnings can be affected by accounting choices or short term factors, the price to sales (P/S) ratio is often used to compare what investors are paying for each dollar of revenue. It is especially common in sectors where margins and reinvestment are important and near term earnings may not tell the whole story.

In general, higher growth and lower risk can support a higher “normal” P/S multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually point to a lower multiple. That is why it helps to look at a few benchmarks rather than a single number.

Arm currently trades on a P/S of 33.82x. This sits above the semiconductor industry average P/S of 5.83x and the peer average of 9.63x. Simply Wall St also provides a Fair Ratio of 46.80x, which is the P/S level suggested after considering factors such as Arm's earnings growth profile, margins, industry, market cap and risk characteristics.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the industry because it adjusts for company specific qualities instead of assuming all semiconductor stocks deserve the same multiple. With the current P/S below the Fair Ratio, this framework points to Arm appearing undervalued on a P/S basis.

One investor might build a cautious Arm Narrative around a Fair Value near the lower analyst and community views of about US$39 to US$70 per share, while another builds a more optimistic Narrative closer to the higher end around US$205 to US$217, and both can clearly see how their different stories lead to very different numbers.

For Arm Holdings however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Arm Holdings Narratives:

Each one takes the same core business and valuation questions you have just seen and pushes them in a different direction, so you can quickly see what has to be true before you would agree with either side.

🐂 Arm Holdings Bull Case

  • Accepts risks around RISC V competitors, customer vertical integration, higher R&D spending, and geopolitical pressures, but still sees a high premium P/E as justified.

data.title: We Think Valeo's (EPA:FR) Solid Earnings Are Understated
data.description:
Shareholders appeared to be happy with Valeo SE's (EPA:FR) solid earnings report last week. According to our analysis of the report, the strong headline profit numbers are supported by strong earnings fundamentals.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

The Impact Of Unusual Items On Profit

For anyone who wants to understand Valeo's profit beyond the statutory numbers, it's important to note that during the last twelve months statutory profit was reduced by €413m due to unusual items. While deductions due to unusual items are disappointing in the first instance, there is a silver lining. We looked at thousands of listed companies and found that unusual items are very often one-off in nature. And that's hardly a surprise given these line items are considered unusual. If Valeo doesn't see those unusual expenses repeat, then all else being equal we'd expect its profit to increase over the coming year.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

Our Take On Valeo's Profit Performance

Because unusual items detracted from Valeo's earnings over the last year, you could argue that we can expect an improved result in the current quarter. Based on this observation, we consider it likely that Valeo's statutory profit actually understates its earnings potential! And on top of that, its earnings per share increased by 23% in the last year. At the end of the day, it's essential to consider more than just the factors above, if you want to understand the company properly. So if you'd like to dive deeper into this stock, it's crucial to consider any risks it's facing. You'd be interested to know, that we found 3 warning signs for Valeo and you'll want to know about these.
